section.title: Paul's Prayer of Thanksgiving
section.sectionRationale: Having poured out praise for the blessings of salvation, Paul now turns that praise into prayer. He does not ask for easier circumstances but for open eyes: that the Ephesians would truly know God and grasp the hope, the inheritance, and the power that are already theirs. That prayer then swells into a fresh vision of the risen and enthroned Christ, seated far above every power and given as head over all things for the church, His body.
section.thesis: Paul thanks God for the Ephesians' faith and love and prays that God would open the eyes of their hearts to know Him, the hope of His calling, the riches of their inheritance, and the surpassing power that raised Christ from the dead and enthroned Him above every power as head over all things for the church, His body.
section.frameNote: Three movements: thanksgiving for their faith and love; the prayer to know Him more, His hope, inheritance, and power; and the great vision of the power that raised and enthroned Christ as head over all for the church.

units.label: Thanksgiving for their faith and love
units.note: Paul does not stop giving thanks

verses.text: For this reason, ever since I heard about your faith in the Lord Jesus and your love for all the saints,
I have not stopped giving thanks for you, remembering you in my prayers,
that the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the glorious Father, may give you a spirit of wisdom and {{apokalypsis|revelation}} in your {{epignosis|knowledge}} of Him.
I ask that the eyes of your heart may be enlightened, so that you may know the {{elpis|hope}} of His calling, the riches of His glorious {{kleronomia|inheritance}} in the saints,
and the surpassing greatness of His {{dynamis|power}} to us who believe. These are in accordance with the working of His mighty strength,

units.label: Christ raised and enthroned, head over all for the church

verses.text: which He exerted in Christ when He raised Him from the dead and seated Him at His right hand in the heavenly realms,
far above all rule and authority, power and dominion, and every name that is named, not only in the present age but also in the one to come.
And God put everything under His feet and made Him {{kephale|head}} over everything for the church,
which is His body, the {{pleroma|fullness}} of Him who fills all in all.

apokalypsis.gloss: Revelation, an unveiling or uncovering of what was hidden. Paul prays not merely for more information but for the Spirit to draw back the veil so the Ephesians truly perceive God. Such knowledge is a gift, given by the Spirit of wisdom and revelation, not achieved by human cleverness alone.

epignosis.gloss: Full or deep knowledge, a knowing that goes beyond bare acquaintance to real, personal recognition. The prefix strengthens the ordinary word for knowledge. Paul's aim is not that they learn about God at a distance but that they know Him truly and intimately, the very heart of what he asks for the church.

elpis.gloss: Hope, not a wishful maybe but a confident expectation grounded in God's promise. The hope of His calling is the certain future to which God has summoned His people. To know this hope is to live with assurance, seeing the destination God has fixed for those He has called.

kleronomia.gloss: Inheritance, the possession passed to an heir. Strikingly, Paul speaks of the riches of His glorious inheritance in the saints, so that believers are God's own treasured inheritance. The word recalls Israel as the LORD's portion and now embraces the whole people God has redeemed as His prized possession.

dynamis.gloss: Power, might, the ability to accomplish. Paul heaps up words for power in verses 19 and 20 to describe the surpassing greatness of God's strength toward believers. It is no abstract force but the very same power that raised Christ from the dead, now at work in those who believe.

kephale.gloss: Head, carrying the sense of both authority over and source or origin. God made the risen Christ head over everything, and gave Him as head for the church, His body. Christ's headship means He rules all things and also supplies life and direction to His people, the body joined to Him.

pleroma.gloss: Fullness, completeness, the full measure. The church is called the fullness of Him who fills all in all, an astonishing phrase. It may mean that the church is that which Christ fills with His presence, or that the church completes Him as the body completes the head; either way it binds Christ and His people together in the closest union.

basic.context: After praising God for salvation, Paul turns to prayer. He thanks God constantly for the Ephesians' faith and love, and then he prays for the one thing they most need: to know God better. He asks that the eyes of their hearts would be opened to see the hope of God's calling, the riches of the inheritance, and the immeasurable power at work in them. That power, he says, is the very power that raised Jesus from the dead and seated Him above every ruler as head over all things for the church.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a prayer, and it teaches us what to pray for. Paul's deepest request is not for comfort or success but for spiritual sight, that believers would grasp what is already true of them in Christ. The prayer flows without a break into a description of Christ's resurrection and enthronement, so that the answer to the prayer is nothing less than a clearer vision of the exalted Lord and His finished work.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul asks God to give a spirit of wisdom and apokalypsis, revelation, an unveiling, in the epignosis, the deep personal knowledge, of God. He prays they would know the elpis, the confident hope, of God's calling, the riches of God's kleronomia, inheritance, in the saints, and the surpassing dynamis, power, toward those who believe. That power raised Christ and made Him kephale, head, over all things for the church, which is His pleroma, fullness.
basic.typology: Christ seated at God's right hand fulfills Psalm 110, where the LORD invites His anointed to sit at His right hand until His enemies are made a footstool. All things put under His feet fulfills Psalm 8, the dominion given to man, now realized in the risen Christ, the true and last Adam who reigns over all creation.
basic.christ: The prayer climaxes in a soaring portrait of Christ: raised from the dead, seated at God's right hand in the heavenly realms, exalted far above every rule, authority, power, and dominion, and above every name in this age and the next. God has put everything under His feet and given Him as head over all things for the church. The exalted Christ is the answer to the church's every need and fear.
basic.god: God is here the glorious Father, the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the source of wisdom and revelation and the one whose mighty strength raised and enthroned Jesus. He is the giver who delights to open blind eyes, and the sovereign who has placed all things under His Son's feet. The same God who wields resurrection power directs it toward those who believe.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 110 pictures the LORD's anointed seated at His right hand. Philippians 2 tells of the name above every name given to the exalted Christ. Colossians 1 names Christ the head of the body, the church, close kin to this passage.
basic.oneStory: The resurrection and enthronement of Christ are the turning point of the whole story. The power that made the world and will remake it has already been unleashed in raising Jesus and seating Him above every power. The church, His body, is joined to this reigning Lord, and the prayer that we would see it clearly is the prayer that we would live in the light of the victory already won.

context.literary: Verses 15 to 23 form a single prayer report, again one long sentence in the Greek. It moves from thanksgiving, verses 15 to 16, to petition, verses 17 to 19, and then, carried by the mention of God's power, into a doxological description of Christ's resurrection and exaltation, verses 20 to 23. The prayer thus flows seamlessly into Christology, the exalted Christ being the very content of what Paul wants them to see.
context.historical: Ephesus was steeped in fear of spiritual powers, magic, and hostile forces named and unnamed. Paul deliberately piles up the vocabulary of power, rule, authority, dominion, and every name that is named, only to declare that the risen Christ is seated far above them all. To a church surrounded by such fears, this enthronement is profoundly good news.
context.canonical: The passage joins the New Testament's great exaltation texts, Philippians 2:9-11, Colossians 1:15-20, and Hebrews 1:3-4, and draws deeply on Psalm 110:1 and Psalm 8:6. It sets Christ's cosmic reign in direct relation to the church, a theme Paul will unfold as the body of Christ throughout the letter.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model and commend prayer for spiritual insight, and to lift the church's eyes to the exalted Christ, so that believers grasp the hope, inheritance, and power that are theirs and take courage that their Lord reigns over every power they fear.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes Paul's prayer and the exaltation of Christ; it does not issue commands. Yet it functions as a pattern for prayer, teaching believers to ask above all for knowledge of God, and it grounds Christian confidence in the accomplished fact of Christ's resurrection and reign.

debates.question: What does it mean that the church is the fullness of Him who fills all in all?

views.label: The church as Christ's complement
views.case: As the body completes the head, so the church is spoken of as the fullness of Christ, the community He fills and through which His presence is expressed, bound to Him in the closest union.

views.label: Christ filling all things
views.case: The emphasis falls on Christ, who fills all in all; the church is the sphere being filled with His fullness, so the phrase magnifies Christ's all-pervading presence rather than any lack in Him.

debates.bearing: Either reading exalts the union of Christ and His church: He needs nothing, yet He has chosen to fill and be joined to His body, giving the church astonishing dignity as the place where His fullness dwells.

debates.question: What is the power Paul prays they would know in verse 19?

views.label: Resurrection power toward believers
views.case: Paul defines the surpassing power by pointing to the resurrection: the same might that raised and enthroned Christ is at work toward those who believe, assuring them of God's strength in their weakness and their own future resurrection.

views.label: Present power for transformed living
views.case: Others stress that this power is already operative now, enabling holy living and perseverance, a foretaste of the resurrection life to come as the letter later urges walking worthily.

debates.bearing: Both hold that God's resurrection power is not distant but directed toward believers, grounding assurance for the future and strength for the present Christian life.

study.originalLanguages: Paul prays that the glorious Father would give a spirit of wisdom and apokalypsis, an unveiling that lets the hidden be seen, deepening their epignosis, their full and personal knowledge of God. He asks that the eyes of the heart be enlightened to grasp three things: the elpis, the confident hope, of God's calling; the riches of His glorious kleronomia, inheritance, in the saints, God's own treasured people; and the surpassing dynamis, power, toward believers. Paul then piles up further words for might to define that power by the resurrection: God raised Christ, seated Him at His right hand, and made Him kephale, head, over everything for the church, which is His body, His pleroma, His fullness, the community filled with and completing the presence of the Lord who fills all in all.

typology.type: Christ seated at God's right hand
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills Psalm 110:1, the LORD's anointed enthroned at His right hand until every enemy is subdued, now realized in the risen Jesus.
Fulfills Psalm 8:6, the dominion given to humanity, now accomplished in Christ the last Adam who reigns over all creation.
Gathers the Old Testament hope of God dwelling among His people into the union of Christ the head with the church His body, filled with His fullness.

study.christ: This passage sets before us the exalted Christ in full glory. Raised bodily from the dead by the Father's mighty power, He is seated at God's right hand in the heavenly realms, the place of supreme honor and authority. He is far above all rule and authority, power and dominion, and above every name that can be named, in this age and the age to come. God has placed everything under His feet and given Him, as reigning Lord over all, to be head of the church, His body. Every power the world fears is beneath His feet, and He rules all things for the good of His people.
study.god: God appears here as the glorious Father, the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, and as the source of every good Paul asks for. He gives the Spirit of wisdom and revelation, opens the eyes of the heart, and pours out the surpassing power that raised Jesus. He is the sovereign who subjected all things to His Son and appointed Him head over all things for the church. This is a God of overwhelming strength who bends that strength toward those who believe.

crossReferences.note: The LORD invites His anointed to sit at His right hand until His enemies are made a footstool, fulfilled in Christ's enthronement.
God exalted Christ and gave Him the name above every name, parallel to His exaltation above every name here.
Christ is the head of the body, the church, the firstborn from the dead, close kin to verses 22 to 23.
Believers are raised and seated with Christ in the heavenly realms, sharing in the exaltation described here.

study.oneStory: The raising and enthroning of Jesus is the hinge on which the whole story turns. The power that spoke creation into being and will one day make all things new has already broken into history in the resurrection, seating the crucified Jesus above every power. The church, His body, is joined to this reigning Lord and shares His victory. Paul's prayer, that we would see this with the eyes of the heart, is the prayer that God's people would live now in the light of the reign that is already secure and will one day be seen by all.

quiz.q: What does Paul chiefly pray for the Ephesians?

options.text: That God would open the eyes of their hearts to know Him more deeply.
options.note: His central request is for spiritual sight and knowledge of God.

options.text: That God would remove all their hardships and troubles.
options.note: A natural wish, but Paul prays for insight, not easier circumstances.

options.text: That they would gain wealth and social standing.
options.note: The riches he names are spiritual, the inheritance and power of God.

options.text: That they would earn God's favor by their efforts.
options.note: The blessings are already theirs; he prays they would see them.

quiz.q: What are the three things Paul wants them to know in verses 18 and 19?

options.text: The hope of His calling, the riches of His inheritance, and His surpassing power.
options.note: These are the three objects of the enlightened heart's knowledge.

options.text: Their giftedness, their reputation, and their comfort.
options.note: The passage names hope, inheritance, and power, not personal status.

options.text: The hope of heaven only, with nothing for the present.
options.note: Hope is one part, but Paul also names the inheritance and present power.

options.text: The rules they must keep to stay saved.
options.note: Paul prays for insight into grace, not a list of requirements.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the power at work toward believers?

options.text: It is the same power that raised Christ and seated Him at God's right hand.
options.note: Verses 19 and 20 define the power by the resurrection and enthronement.

options.text: It is a vague, impersonal force in the universe.
options.note: It is God's own mighty strength, shown in raising Jesus.

options.text: It is a power only some elite believers can access.
options.note: Paul says it is toward us who believe, all who trust Christ.

options.text: It is a power that has not yet begun to work.
options.note: It has already worked mightily in raising Christ from the dead.

quiz.q: Where is the risen Christ seated, and over what?

options.text: At God's right hand, far above every rule, authority, power, and dominion.
options.note: Verses 20 and 21 place Him above every power and name.

options.text: Among the ranks of the angels as one power among many.
options.note: He is far above all such powers, not one among them.

options.text: In authority over this present age but not the age to come.
options.note: He reigns above every name in this age and the one to come.

options.text: In a waiting place until He earns His throne.
options.note: He is already enthroned with everything under His feet.

quiz.q: What relationship does the passage draw between Christ and the church?

options.text: He is head over all things for the church, which is His body.
options.note: Verses 22 and 23 name Christ the head and the church His body.

options.text: The church rules over Christ as its servant.
options.note: Christ is the head; the church is His body under Him.

options.text: The church is unrelated to Christ's cosmic reign.
options.note: His reign over all things is precisely for the church.

options.text: The church is only loosely connected to Christ.
options.note: The union is intimate: body to head, filled with His fullness.

quiz.q: What does it mean that the church is His fullness?

options.text: The church is bound to Christ in closest union as the body filled with His presence.
options.note: Fullness binds Christ and His people in intimate union.

options.text: The church supplies something Christ truly lacked.
options.note: Christ needs nothing; He graciously chooses to fill and join His body.

options.text: The church replaces Christ as the source of life.
options.note: Christ remains the head; the church is filled by Him, not a substitute.

options.text: The phrase has no real meaning for believers.
options.note: It gives the church astonishing dignity as the place His fullness dwells.

questions: Paul's deepest prayer for his friends is that they would know God better. When you pray for people you love, how often do you ask for their spiritual sight rather than only their circumstances?
Paul asks that the eyes of your heart be enlightened. Where do you need God to open your eyes to a hope, an inheritance, or a power that is already yours in Christ?
The same power that raised Jesus from the dead is at work toward you. How would facing this week change if you really believed resurrection power was available to you?
Christ is seated far above every rule and authority and every name that is named. What powers or fears in your life need to be seen in the light of His reign over them all?
You belong to the body of the Christ who fills all in all. How does being joined to the reigning Lord shape the way you see the church and your place in it?

translationNotes.note: A spirit of wisdom and revelation may refer to the Holy Spirit who gives these gifts or to a disposition He works; either way the wisdom and revelation are God's gift, not human achievement.
His glorious inheritance in the saints most likely means God's own inheritance, that is, His people as His treasured possession, though some read it as the inheritance God gives to the saints.
The fullness of Him who fills all in all can be read actively, the church as that which Christ fills, or as the church completing Him as body to head; the Greek allows both and stresses their union.

meta.notes: BSB text for Ephesians 1:15-23 reproduced as supplied. No dashes were present to convert. Inline tokens mark the seven supplied lexicon keys, each used once.
